lesson.menu_desc: Customising your order & describing food -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- The Art of Dining Out
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 9 -- The Art of Dining Out

reading.rtitle: Slow Food, Real Places: Why the Best Meals Are Never Rushed
paras: Ask any seasoned traveller where they truly felt at home abroad, and the answer is rarely a famous monument. More often, it is a table. It might be a tiny family-run trattoria where the owner insists on having the pasta made fresh, or a market stall where a cook gets the fish grilled to order in front of you. Food, more than sightseeing, is how we read a culture from the inside.

The trouble is that tourists are often served a hollow version of this. The menus are translated into six languages, the portions are huge but bland, and the savoury depth that locals take for granted is quietly stripped away. A meal that should be hearty and memorable becomes a transaction. The clever traveller learns to step off the main square, to ask where the staff themselves eat, and to be a little adventurous with the menu.

There is also an art to letting yourself be looked after. In the best places you do not order so much as surrender: you let the kitchen whip up whatever is freshest, you have your wine chosen for you, and you splurge, just once, on the dish the chef is proudest of. The reward is not only a fuller stomach but a sharper sense of where, exactly, in the world you are.
reading.source: Adapted from a food & travel essay, The Slow Journal

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main argument?
choices: Famous monuments teach you more about a culture than its food does.
A real local meal reveals a culture better than sightseeing, if you avoid the tourist version.
Tourist restaurants with translated menus are usually the safest choice.

items: The writer says travellers usually feel most at home in front of famous monuments.
f
The answer is rarely a monument; it is more often a table.
Tourist meals are often big but lacking in flavour.
t
The portions are huge but bland, and the savoury depth is stripped away.
The writer recommends asking where the staff themselves eat.
t
The clever traveller asks where the staff eat and steps off the main square.
In the best places, the writer thinks you should control every detail of your order.
f
You do not order so much as surrender and let the kitchen decide.

followup.text: Notice how the writer keeps describing food being done by someone else: the pasta made fresh, the fish grilled to order, your wine chosen for you. That is exactly the grammar of this lesson -- the causative.

bank.label: Ordering & reacting at the table
items: Could we get the fish grilled rather than fried?
I'd like to have the steak cooked medium, please.
What would you recommend if we're feeling adventurous?
Could you have this boxed up for us to take away?
This is so succulent -- my compliments to the chef!
Scenario 1
The waiter brings a dish that is far too bland for your taste. Send it back politely, using the causative ('Could I have it...?').
Scenario 2
You are peckish but not starving. Ask the waiter to recommend something light, and order it.
Scenario 3
It is a special occasion and you decide to splurge. Tell your companion you will let the chef whip up the tasting menu.
Scenario 4
You cannot finish such a generous portion. Ask the waiter to have the rest boxed up.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each sentence using the causative (have / get something done)
items: A waiter grilled the fish for us.
had
I'm asking the kitchen to cook my steak medium.
get
Someone is boxing up our leftovers right now.
having
The chef prepared a special dish for the table.
had
I'll ask them to swap the side dish.
get

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: We had the fish grilled (by a waiter).
I'm getting my steak cooked medium.
We're having our leftovers boxed up right now.
We had a special dish prepared for the table.
I'll get the side dish swapped.
answerkey.note: Form check: have / get + object + past participle. The focus is on the result, not on who does it.

listenings.text: Good evening, and welcome. Before you choose, let me run you through tonight's specials. To start, we have a hearty pumpkin soup, beautifully savoury, with a generous portion of toasted seeds on top. For the main course, the chef is doing a succulent slow-roasted lamb that the whole table tends to fight over. Now, if you happen to have any dietary needs, do let me know. We can easily get the lamb prepared without the garlic butter, and we can have the risotto made completely dairy-free. The portions are quite large tonight, so if you are only feeling a little peckish, two starters often work better than a full main.

Honestly, dining out has become one of my favourite parts of travelling. When I was younger, I would just eat whatever was put in front of me. These days I am far more confident. If a dish comes with something I do not fancy, I simply ask to have it swapped. If the kitchen can cater for a craving, they usually will. The other night the portion was so generous that I could not finish it, and the food was such a treat that I asked the waiter to get the rest boxed up to take away. A good meal abroad is no longer something I am nervous about. It is something I look forward to.

extra_audio.text: First, feeling peckish, she chose a restaurant that catered for her cravings. Then she had the steak cooked rare and asked to swap the fries for a salad. The portion was so generous that she could not finish it. She had the leftovers boxed up and the chef whipped up a savoury treat. Finally, she splurged on dessert because the meal was such a success.
